title: Are Early Artifact Forecasts Actionable for Membership Privacy? A Budget-Matched Study of Selective Intervention
description: This paper is positioned as a narrow empirical test, not a new defense family. It studies whether early artifact forecasts can drive a fixed 10% selective smoothing budget that reduces forecast-conditioned worst-decile membership leakage and privacy disparity better than random targeting, loss-only targeting, or global smoothing under a single-GPU budget.
motivation: Recent work already shows that membership privacy risk is heterogeneous across records, often forecastable early, and worth evaluating through disparity-sensitive metrics. What remains unclear is whether those forecasts are actually actionable: under a tightly controlled budget, do they beat simpler targeting rules strongly enough to justify an online selective intervention policy?
proposed_approach: Run a 15% ERM warm-up while logging LT-IQR, mean loss, inverse margin, and prediction flips for each record. Convert those signals into an equal-weight percentile-rank forecast score, refresh the top-risk 10% every 4 epochs with EMA smoothing, and apply same-class vicinal smoothing only to that subset; compare against ERM, global mixup, RelaxLoss, random targeting, and loss-only targeting using the exact same intervention budget, three seeds, and the same privacy metrics. Evaluate privacy with global-variance LiRA-lite plus a stronger class-conditional LiRA-lite robustness check, while explicitly limiting claims to lightweight black-box score-based attacks.

related_work.title: Free Record-Level Privacy Risk Evaluation Through Artifact-Based Methods
related_work.how_it_differs: Pollock et al. show that training artifacts can estimate record-level privacy risk without shadow-model auditing. This proposal treats that as input and asks whether such forecasts are actionable enough to drive a fixed online intervention.

related_work.title: Evaluating the Dynamics of Membership Privacy in Deep Learning
related_work.how_it_differs: Chen et al. show that vulnerable records can often be identified well before convergence. This proposal turns that observation into a pre-registered matched-budget comparison between forecast-driven and simpler targeting rules.

related_work.title: Understanding Membership Inferences on Well-Generalized Learning Models
related_work.how_it_differs: Long et al. are an important precursor for instance-level vulnerability analysis: they show that well-generalized models still contain individually vulnerable records and propose ways to identify them for attack. This proposal does not claim to discover heterogeneity; it tests whether lightweight artifact forecasts can support intervention under a fixed training budget.

related_work.title: MIST: Defending Against Membership Inference Attacks Through Membership-Invariant Subspace Training
related_work.how_it_differs: MIST is the closest standard-training defense because it explicitly focuses protection on vulnerable instances. This proposal narrows its contribution accordingly: it studies whether a much simpler forecast-triggered policy is worthwhile as a budget-matched empirical baseline.

related_work.title: MIAShield: Defending Membership Inference Attacks via Preemptive Exclusion of Members
related_work.how_it_differs: MIAShield is relevant proactive privacy-aware training and inference-time prior art, including learning-based exclusion oracles. This proposal differs mainly in scope and protocol: it evaluates an online training-time trigger with fixed budget and much lighter implementation cost.

related_work.title: AdaMixup: A Dynamic Defense Framework for Membership Inference Attack Mitigation
related_work.how_it_differs: AdaMixup adapts mixup strength during training, but not through a pre-registered record-level forecast-and-target protocol. This proposal asks whether forecast-conditioned targeting helps more than dynamic global smoothing under the same runtime regime.

related_work.title: RelaxLoss: Defending Membership Inference Attacks without Losing Utility
related_work.how_it_differs: RelaxLoss is a lightweight global defense with negligible implementation friction. It is included as the strongest guaranteed global baseline for the privacy-gain-per-runtime comparison.

related_work.title: Membership Inference Attacks From First Principles
related_work.how_it_differs: Carlini et al. motivate low-FPR likelihood-ratio attacks as the relevant stress test. This proposal adopts that evaluation frame and adds a class-conditional calibration check beyond the cheapest LiRA-lite approximation.

related_work.title: Membership Inference Attacks as Privacy Tools: Reliability, Disparity and Ensemble
related_work.how_it_differs: Wang et al. argue that privacy evaluation should emphasize reliability and disparity. This proposal adopts that perspective and pre-registers forecast-conditioned worst-decile leakage and privacy disparity as primary outcomes.

hypothesis: A non-learned aggregation of early training artifacts can forecast future privacy outliers well enough that online selective same-class smoothing improves forecast-conditioned worst-decile leakage and privacy disparity more than random targeting, loss-only targeting, or global smoothing under the same 10% intervention budget, while offering a competitive privacy-gain-per-runtime tradeoff against lightweight global defenses.
success_criteria: On both Purchase100 and CIFAR-10, forecast-driven targeting improves forecast-conditioned worst-decile leakage or privacy disparity over ERM and global mixup with no more than 1.5 absolute percentage points of test-accuracy loss.
Forecast-driven targeting outperforms both random targeting and loss-only targeting under the same 10% intervention budget and the same refresh-every-4-epochs protocol.

Forecast quality is non-trivial: the warm-up forecast score has positive Spearman correlation with the final attack score, and Precision@10 for identifying final top-decile privacy outliers exceeds the random 10% baseline by a meaningful margin.

The privacy advantage remains visible under both global-variance LiRA-lite and a stronger class-conditional LiRA-lite calibration, while the paper explicitly limits claims to lightweight black-box score-based attacks.
Forecast-driven targeting achieves better privacy gain per extra training minute than at least one guaranteed global baseline, preferably RelaxLoss.

If code reuse is cheap, AdaMixup or MIST can be included as contingent external baselines; if not, the contribution remains a budget-matched mechanism study rather than a novelty-driven defense claim.
